questions.specification: A library management system tracks books, authors, members, loans, and reservations.

parts.content:
Create an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram analysis for the library system by completing the missing components in the table below:

| Entity | Primary Key | Attributes | Relationships | Cardinality (between entities) | Business Rules |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Book | BookID | — | — | — | — |
| Author | — | AuthorName, Biography | Writes Book | Many-to-Many (Author–Book) | — |
| Member | MemberID | — | Borrows books | — | Max 5 books per member |
| Loan | — | — | — | — | — |
| Reservation | — | ReservationDate | Member reserves Book | One-to-Many (Member–Reservation) | Max 3 reservations |
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the attributes and relationships for the Book entity (e.g., Title, ISBN, PublicationYear, Genre, written by authors, borrowed by members). <marks>2</marks>
- **Identify** the primary key and relationships for the Author entity, specifically AuthorID and the relationship to writing books. <marks>2</marks>
- **Identify** the missing attributes and relationships for the Member entity (e.g., Name, Email, Phone, JoinDate, makes reservations). <marks>2</marks>
- **Identify** the primary key and attributes for the Loan entity (e.g., LoanID, LoanDate, DueDate, ReturnDate) which link members to books. <marks>2</marks>
- **Identify** the primary key, additional attributes, and business rules for the Reservation entity, such as ReservationID and queue ordering. <marks>2</marks>

parts.content: Analyze how normalization to Third Normal Form ($3\text{NF}$) would improve this library database design and identify potential denormalization decisions for performance optimization.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** how $3\text{NF}$ eliminates transitive dependencies, for example, by separating author details into a separate table from book records. <marks>1</marks>
- **Identify** how normalization reduces data redundancy and improves data integrity when authors have multiple books. <marks>1</marks>
- **Discuss** a potential denormalization decision, such as storing the author's name directly in the Book table to reduce the need for joins in frequent queries. <marks>1</marks>
- **Analyze** the trade-off between the gain in query performance and the loss of storage efficiency or data consistency risks. <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification:
A healthcare analytics platform processes patient data from multiple hospitals for population health studies.

Which action best describes an appropriate way to anonymise patient data for research while keeping it useful for statistical analysis?

options.content: Remove direct identifiers (e.g. names, national identification numbers) and replace them with unique research IDs.
options.markscheme: Correct: removes direct identifiers and uses a non-identifying key so records can still be linked for analysis.

options.content: Create a stored procedure that applies k-anonymity by generalising quasi-identifiers such as birth dates and postal codes.
options.markscheme: Too advanced/out of scope for SL Paper 1; focuses on a specific advanced technique and implementation detail rather than the core anonymisation action.

options.content: Maintain statistical validity by adding random noise to every data field in the dataset.
options.markscheme: Adding noise to every field is not an appropriate general approach; it can unnecessarily damage data utility and is not required for basic anonymisation.

options.content: Encrypt all patient data so researchers can run queries without decrypting it.
options.markscheme: Encryption protects confidentiality in storage/transit but does not by itself anonymise data for research use.

questions.specification:
A manufacturing company tracks products, suppliers, purchase orders, and inventory levels.

Which statement best describes how a database view can help in this manufacturing environment?

options.content: A view stores a physical copy of the underlying data so queries run faster even if the base tables change.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: a standard view is virtual and does not store a physical copy of the data.

options.content: A view can present data from one or more tables as a single virtual table, simplifying complex queries and limiting what data a user can see.
options.markscheme: Correct: views encapsulate joins/calculations and can restrict rows/columns exposed to users.

options.content: A view automatically enforces referential integrity between products and suppliers without needing primary or foreign keys.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: referential integrity is enforced by keys/constraints, not by views.

options.content: A view replaces the need for indexes because it always makes queries on purchase orders faster.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: views do not replace indexes and do not guarantee faster performance.

questions.specification: A smart city initiative integrates multiple systems including traffic management, utility monitoring, and citizen services.

parts.content: In words (no SQL required), describe a relational database schema (tables, key fields, and relationships) that can store readings from many different sensor types and also allow new sensor types to be added without changing the database table structure.
parts.markscheme:
- A generic **Sensor** (or Device) table to store sensor identity and fixed attributes, e.g. $sensorId$ (PK), location, etc. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A **SensorType** (or Category) table to define types, e.g. $typeId$ (PK), name/description, with **Sensor** referencing **SensorType** via a foreign key. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A **Reading** (or Measurement) table to store captured values, e.g. $readingId$ (PK), $sensorId$ (FK), timestamp, value (and optionally a unit/metric field). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation that new sensor types are added by inserting a new row in **SensorType** (and linking sensors to it) rather than altering the schema/tables. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A financial trading platform processes millions of transactions daily and requires reliable database operations. The platform's engineers are reviewing core relational database design and SQL techniques to ensure accurate and efficient access to financial data.

parts.content:
Analyze different relational database design and SQL techniques by completing the following table:

| Technique | Implementation Method | Purpose/Impact | Use Case | Potential Drawbacks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normalization | — | Reduce redundancy and improve data integrity | — | More tables and joins |
| Primary and Foreign Keys | Primary key and foreign key constraints | — | — | Constraint violations if data is inconsistent |
| Indexes | — | Faster searching/filtering on indexed columns | — | Storage overhead and slower inserts/updates |
| Query Design (Joins) | Use appropriate joins and select only needed columns | — | Combining related tables | —
parts.markscheme:
- **Normalization**: Method: apply 1NF, 2NF, 3NF; Use Case: designing transaction/customer/instrument tables to avoid repeating groups and partial/transitive dependencies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Normalization**: Purpose/Impact: reduces redundancy and improves data integrity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Primary and Foreign Keys**: Purpose/Impact: enforce entity integrity and referential integrity between related tables. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Primary and Foreign Keys**: Use Case: link `Transactions` to `Customers`/`Accounts`/`Instruments` using foreign keys. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Indexes**: Method: create an index on columns used in `WHERE`/`JOIN` conditions (e.g., B-tree index). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Indexes**: Use Case: frequent lookups/filtering such as searching transactions by `customer_id` or `trade_date`. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Query Design (Joins)**: Purpose/Impact: returns correct results efficiently by joining on keys and selecting only required columns/rows. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Query Design (Joins)**: Potential Drawbacks: poorly chosen joins or missing conditions can cause large intermediate results/slow queries (or incorrect duplicates). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how normalization (up to 3NF) improves a database for a high-transaction environment.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces data redundancy by storing each fact once (avoids repeated data). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves data integrity/consistency by reducing update anomalies (insert/update/delete anomalies). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes relationships between entities clearer via well-structured tables and key dependencies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Trade-off: can require more joins, which may increase query complexity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A gaming platform tracks player statistics, achievements, and leaderboards with real-time updates.

parts.content: Compare different approaches to handling frequent updates to player scores and rankings, including client-side vs server-side validation and the use of atomic database operations.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** client-side validation, noting it provides immediate feedback to users but cannot be trusted on its own because it can be bypassed or manipulated <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** server-side validation, noting it is authoritative and enforces rules consistently but may add latency compared with client-side checks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that gaming applications typically use both: client-side validation for responsiveness and server-side validation for security and correctness <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outline** the use of atomic operations such as $\text{INCREMENT}$ to ensure that individual score updates are applied reliably without overwriting each other <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Compare** the approaches by stating at least one advantage and one disadvantage of each in this context (e.g., responsiveness vs trustworthiness; reduced errors vs increased processing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Apply** the comparison to leaderboards by explaining how consistent server-side checks and atomic updates help keep rankings accurate <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how batch processing techniques can efficiently update aggregated statistics and leaderboards.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** how batch processing can be used to aggregate multiple score updates, thereby reducing the total number of operations and the load on the database <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** the role of scheduled jobs in recalculating leaderboards during off-peak hours to maintain performance during high-traffic periods <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
An online learning platform manages courses, instructors, students, enrollments, and assessments.

Which option best describes how different ER relationship types are implemented when converting a conceptual ER model to a logical relational schema?

options.content: One-to-one: always create a junction table; One-to-many: create a junction table; Many-to-many: place a foreign key on either side; Recursive: cannot be represented in a relational schema.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: one-to-one and one-to-many do not require junction tables, and recursive relationships can be represented using a self-referencing foreign key.

options.content: One-to-one: merge tables or place a foreign key on either side (consider optionality); One-to-many: place a foreign key in the child table (enforce referential integrity); Many-to-many: create a junction table (often with a composite primary key and any relationship attributes); Recursive: use a self-referencing foreign key (consider hierarchy depth and cycles).
options.markscheme: Correct: matches standard ER-to-relational mapping strategies, including optionality for one-to-one, child-side foreign key for one-to-many, junction table for many-to-many with possible relationship attributes, and self-referencing foreign key for recursive relationships.

options.content: One-to-one: place foreign keys in both tables; One-to-many: place foreign keys in both tables; Many-to-many: store repeated values in a single column; Recursive: duplicate rows to represent the hierarchy.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: placing foreign keys in both tables is not required and can introduce redundancy; repeated values in a column violates normalisation; duplicating rows is not a standard or safe approach for hierarchies.

options.content: One-to-one: always merge tables; One-to-many: place foreign key in the parent table; Many-to-many: add a multivalued attribute to one table; Recursive: represent it only with application code, not in the database.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: one-to-one is not always merged (optional/mandatory participation matters); one-to-many foreign key belongs in the child, not the parent; many-to-many requires a junction table; recursive relationships are representable in the schema via a self-referencing foreign key.

questions.specification: A real estate management system handles properties, agents, clients, transactions, and market valuations.

parts.content:
Evaluate the following design approaches for a flexible database schema that can accommodate different property types (residential, commercial, industrial) with varying attributes. For each approach, describe an implementation method and state at least one advantage, one disadvantage, and when it is most suitable.

| Design approach | Implementation method | Advantages | Disadvantages | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Table Inheritance |  |  |  |  |
| Class Table Inheritance |  |  |  |  |
| Concrete Table Inheritance |  |  |  |  |
| JSON/NoSQL Hybrid |  |  |  |  |
parts.markscheme:
- Single Table Inheritance: implementation includes a single table with a type discriminator column and nullable columns for type-specific attributes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Single Table Inheritance: advantage such as simple schema and simple queries (no joins) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Single Table Inheritance: disadvantage such as many $NULL$ values and/or constraints harder to enforce for type-specific fields <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Single Table Inheritance: suitable when property types are similar and share many attributes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Class Table Inheritance: implementation includes a base table for common attributes plus separate type-specific tables linked by primary/foreign key <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Class Table Inheritance: advantage such as normalized design and fewer $NULL$ values <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Class Table Inheritance: disadvantage such as complex joins and potentially slower reads for full objects <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Concrete Table Inheritance OR JSON/NoSQL Hybrid: either (a) Concrete Table: advantage no joins needed per type but disadvantage duplicated common columns; suitable for very distinct property types, OR (b) JSON/NoSQL: implementation uses flexible document/JSON column for varying attributes; advantage schema evolution; disadvantage limited query/indexing/constraint capabilities compared with relational; suitable for evolving requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze how geographic information systems (GIS) integration affects database design decisions for property location data.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of spatial data types to store coordinates and geometries (e.g., points for properties, polygons for parcels/land boundaries) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of spatial indexes to optimize location-based queries (e.g., proximity, within-area, intersection) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Need for coordinate reference system (CRS) / projection standardization and consistent handling of transformations for integration with mapping services <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Performance/storage considerations for complex spatial calculations (e.g., buffering, area computations) and implications for query design <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A healthcare clinic designs a database for patient records, appointments, treatments, and medical history.

parts.content: Outline six features of a database design for a healthcare clinic that address privacy requirements, audit trails, and regulatory compliance.
parts.markscheme:
- Separate personally identifiable information (PII) from medical data for privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implement role-based access control through user permissions tables <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use audit tables to track all data modifications with user IDs and timestamps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Define encryption requirements for sensitive medical data during storage and transmission <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Establish data retention policies to enforce regulatory compliance periods <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include patient consent tracking for data usage and sharing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how date/time fields and logging tables can track changes in patient medical conditions over time and support historical reporting requirements.
parts.markscheme:
- Store a date/time for each condition record (for example, diagnosis date and/or start/end dates) so each entry is time-stamped <marks>1 mark</marks>
- New updates are added as new rows (rather than overwriting), preserving the sequence of changes to the condition over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A history/log table records changes (for example, old value, new value, user ID, timestamp), supporting an audit trail <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Time-stamped data enables historical reporting (for example, viewing a patient’s condition list at a given date or producing trend reports across visits) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A content management system handles articles, user comments, and search functionality across multiple languages.

parts.content: Design a database solution for search capabilities that supports multiple languages and ranking results by relevance.
parts.markscheme:
- Propose storing articles (and related metadata such as title, language, publication date) in appropriate relational tables with suitable keys and relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify appropriate indexes to support searching, e.g. indexing the columns used for searching/filtering (such as title/keywords and language) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how to support multiple languages in the database, e.g. a language field/code for each article (or separate translation records linked to an article) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how the language information can be used to restrict searches to a chosen language or to return results grouped/filtered by language <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe a simple relevance ranking method based on database-stored data, e.g. ordering by number of keyword matches or whether terms appear in the title vs body, possibly using weighted fields <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outline ordering results using SQL (e.g. ORDER BY on a computed score and/or secondary sort such as most recent publication date) to present the most relevant items first <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze how database cursors and pagination techniques handle large result sets efficiently in web applications.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how pagination limits the number of rows returned per request to reduce load and response time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how using large OFFSET values can become inefficient because the database still has to skip/scan many rows before returning the page <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify an alternative approach that avoids large offsets, e.g. using a WHERE condition based on the last seen indexed value (such as an ID or date) to fetch the next page <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State the importance of maintaining consistent ordering/criteria (and handling concurrent inserts/deletes) so users do not see missing/duplicated results between pages <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A library management system tracks books, authors, members, loans, and reservations.

Create an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram **or** a clearly structured ER analysis table for the library system.

parts.content:
Entity
Primary Key
Attributes
Relationships
Cardinality
Business Rules
Book
BookID



Author


AuthorName, Biography


Many-to-Many


Member
MemberID


Borrows books


Max 5 books per member
Loan




Reservation


ReservationDate


One-to-Many
Max 3 reservations
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies suitable entities and keys consistent with the prompt (e.g., Book with BookID; Member with MemberID; Loan with LoanID; Reservation with ReservationID; Author with AuthorID or equivalent) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides appropriate Book attributes (e.g., Title, ISBN, PublicationYear, Genre) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides appropriate Author attributes including AuthorName and Biography (and may include others) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides appropriate Member attributes (e.g., Name, Email, Phone, JoinDate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models Book–Author as a Many-to-Many relationship (e.g., WrittenBy/Writes) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resolves the Many-to-Many relationship using an associative entity/table (e.g., BookAuthor with BookID, AuthorID as composite key/foreign keys) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models borrowing via Loan as an associative relationship between Member and Book (each Loan links one Member and one Book; Member/Book can have many Loans over time) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Includes the business rule: Max 5 books per member (e.g., constraint on concurrent active Loans per Member) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models Reservation as One-to-Many as given (e.g., one Member to many Reservations, or one Book to many Reservations, with clear interpretation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Includes the business rule: Max 3 reservations (e.g., constraint on active Reservations per Member and/or per Book, consistent with the chosen interpretation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: (b) Analyze how normalization to Third Normal Form (3NF) would improve this library database design and identify potential denormalization decisions for performance optimization.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains that 3NF removes transitive dependencies so non-key attributes depend only on the key (e.g., separating repeating descriptive data such as AuthorName/Biography into Author, and using an associative table for Book–Author) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States that 3NF reduces redundancy and update/insert/delete anomalies (e.g., one author’s details stored once even if linked to many books) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a plausible denormalization for performance (e.g., storing commonly queried derived/summary data such as current number of active loans for a member, or caching author names for search) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Discusses trade-off: improved query speed vs increased storage and risk of inconsistency/extra update logic <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A financial trading system requires ultra-low latency data access and high availability for real-time decision making.

parts.content: Design a database architecture that minimizes query latency while ensuring data consistency for trading operations.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe using appropriate indexing (for example, indexing by timestamp and/or instrument) to speed up queries <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe using caching (for example, keeping most frequently accessed data in memory) to reduce repeated database reads <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe using replication across multiple servers to improve availability (if one server fails, another can serve requests) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe using transactions / ACID properties to ensure consistent updates (for example, atomic commit and rollback on failure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: An e-commerce platform experiences rapid growth requiring database scalability and performance optimization.

parts.content: Explain how indexing and database normalization can improve database performance under high load.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that indexing speeds up data retrieval by allowing faster searches on key fields (e.g. reducing full table scans) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that normalization reduces data redundancy and improves data integrity/consistency, which can reduce unnecessary storage and update anomalies (supporting efficient updates under load) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A customer relationship management (CRM) system requires complex reporting and data analysis capabilities.

*Paper: ib-2*

parts.content: Compare different approaches to database programming including embedded SQL, stored procedures, and ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) frameworks.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** Embedded SQL as the direct integration of SQL statements within application source code; it is straightforward for simple queries but can lead to high coupling and maintenance challenges. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that stored procedures are logic stored and executed on the database server, which improves network performance and security though they are typically database-vendor specific and less portable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) frameworks provide an object-oriented abstraction layer that improves developer productivity and portability, though they may introduce performance overhead for complex reporting tasks. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Make a relevant comparative point** about coupling/maintainability between at least two approaches. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Make a relevant comparative point** about performance (server-side execution vs application-side execution vs ORM overhead) between at least two approaches. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Make a relevant comparative point** about portability/vendor lock-in between at least two approaches. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Write pseudocode for a stored procedure that calculates a monthly sales performance metric (total sales for the given month and year), including error handling and transaction management.
parts.markscheme:
```
PROCEDURE CalculateSalesMetrics(targetMonth, targetYear)
    TRY
        START TRANSACTION
        
        INSERT INTO monthly_metrics
            SELECT targetMonth, targetYear, SUM(sales_amount)
            FROM orders
            WHERE order_month = targetMonth AND order_year = targetYear
            
        COMMIT
    CATCH
        ROLLBACK
        OUTPUT "Error: Metric calculation failed."
    END TRY
END PROCEDURE
```

- **Identify** the use of an exception handler (e.g., `TRY/CATCH`) to manage potential errors. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** the implementation of transaction management (e.g., `START`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`) to ensure data integrity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outline** the correct calculation logic, including parameter handling and the use of an aggregate function (e.g., `SUM`). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** the appropriate overall procedure structure and declaration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A smart city initiative integrates multiple systems including traffic management, utility monitoring, and citizen services.

parts.content: Outline, in prose, a flexible relational database design for storing sensor readings so that **new sensor types can be added with minimal changes** (you may include a small table sketch).
parts.markscheme:
- Use a core entity for sensors (e.g. Sensor) and a separate entity for readings (e.g. Reading) linked by a key, so new devices/types can be added as new records rather than new tables/columns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include a SensorType (or Category) table to classify sensors; each Sensor references a SensorType via a foreign key. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Store measurement definitions as data (e.g. MeasurementType/Attribute with units) rather than hard-coded columns, and link readings to the measurement definition (e.g. Reading has MeasurementTypeID + value). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that adding a new sensor type involves inserting new rows in the type/attribute tables (and then storing readings against them), not altering the database schema. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: An online learning platform manages courses, instructors, students, enrollments, and assessments.

parts.content:
Evaluate the process of converting a conceptual ER model to a logical relational schema, discussing how different relationship types are handled: 

| Relationship Type | Implementation Strategy | Example Scenario | Foreign Key Placement | Additional Considerations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| One-to-One ($1:1$) | — | Instructor-Profile | — | — |
| One-to-Many ($1:N$) | Foreign key in child | Course-Enrollment | In child table | — |
| Many-to-Many ($M:N$) | Junction table | Student-Course | — | Junction table attributes |
| Recursive | Self-referencing | Course prerequisites | — | — |
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest merging tables or placing a foreign key on either side depending on participation; discuss the importance of handling optional relationships to avoid null values <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify a valid example scenario such as the relationship between a Course and its Enrollments; explain the requirement for referential integrity constraints <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that a junction table is used to resolve the relationship; discuss the use of a composite primary key and additional relationship attributes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe the implementation of a self-referencing foreign key; discuss considerations regarding hierarchy depth and cycle prevention <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze the challenges of designing databases for flexible course structures where courses may have varying assessment types and grading schemes.
parts.markscheme:
- Outline how separate tables (or related tables) for Assessment and AssessmentType can accommodate different assessment methods across courses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how optional relationships and nullable foreign keys may be needed when not all courses use the same set of assessments or grading components <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify the need for validation rules/constraints (for example, ensuring assessment weights for a course sum to $100\%$) to maintain data integrity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Discuss the trade-offs between making the schema flexible and maintaining simple queries/reporting and consistent data <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A healthcare clinic designs a database for patient records, appointments, treatments, and medical history.

parts.content: Describe a database design approach and key features that address privacy requirements, audit trails, and regulatory compliance. You do not need to draw an ER diagram; focus on design decisions and controls you would include.
parts.markscheme:
- Separate personally identifiable information (PII) from medical data for privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implement role-based access control through user permissions tables <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Audit tables track all data modifications with user and timestamp information <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encryption requirements for sensitive medical data storage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Data retention policies enforce regulatory compliance periods <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Patient consent tracking for data usage and sharing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how a database can track changes in a patient’s medical conditions over time and support historical reporting (for example, viewing what was recorded at a past date).
parts.markscheme:
- Store dated records/versions of condition data (eg start/end dates or effective dates) rather than overwriting values <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Support queries for a specific date/time period (point-in-time view of the patient record) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Keep a history of updates/changes (eg change log or history table linked to patient/condition) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables trend analysis/longitudinal reporting of patient health outcomes over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A manufacturing company tracks products, suppliers, purchase orders, and inventory levels.

Which database feature is primarily used to simplify complex queries by presenting data from multiple tables as a single virtual table?

options.markscheme: Correct: a view is a stored query that presents data as a virtual table, simplifying complex joins/calculations.
Incorrect: a primary key uniquely identifies records but does not simplify multi-table queries as a virtual table.
Incorrect: an index improves query performance but does not present combined data as a virtual table.
Incorrect: a trigger runs automatically on data changes; it is not used to present a simplified virtual table for querying.

questions.specification: An inventory management system needs to handle real-time stock updates and generate automated alerts.

parts.content: Explain how database triggers can automate business logic and maintain data consistency, providing specific examples for inventory management.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that an `AFTER INSERT` trigger on a sales table automatically updates inventory quantities so that stock levels reflect recent transactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that a `BEFORE UPDATE` trigger can validate stock levels to prevent negative inventory, maintaining data consistency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that an `AFTER UPDATE` trigger can automatically generate alerts or reorder requests when stock levels fall below specified reorder points <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain a further relevant trigger-based example linked to inventory management (e.g., an `AFTER DELETE` trigger to reverse stock changes when a sale is cancelled; a trigger to write audit logs for stock adjustments; a trigger to enforce that stock movements are recorded in a stock-movement table) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes clear how automation occurs (trigger fires implicitly on table events) and links this to maintaining consistency across related tables (e.g., sales and inventory) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses specific, correct inventory-focused detail (tables/fields/conditions) rather than generic statements <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the potential drawbacks of using triggers extensively and alternative approaches for implementing business rules.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that extensive use of triggers can make debugging difficult and create "hidden" business logic that executes implicitly, reducing transparency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State an alternative approach such as implementing business rules in the application layer, which provides better visibility and ease of testing <marks>1 mark</marks>
